section.title: Living to Please God
section.sectionRationale: Turning from thanksgiving to exhortation, Paul urges the church to excel in a life that pleases God. He grounds it in God's will for their sanctification: sexual purity that honors God and neighbor, brotherly love that overflows, and quiet, honest, diligent living that commends the gospel to outsiders.
section.thesis: God's will for His people is their sanctification: to abandon the impurity of the surrounding world for sexual holiness that honors God and does not wrong others, and to grow in brotherly love and quiet, industrious living, because the God who calls them gives them His Holy Spirit.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to please God more and more; sanctification in sexual purity as God's will, backed by His judgment and His Spirit; and love, quiet living, and honest work that adorn the faith before outsiders.

verses.text: Finally, brothers, we ask and encourage you in the Lord Jesus to live in a way that is {{aresko|pleasing}} to God, just as you have received from us. This is how you already live, so you should do so all the more.
For you know the instructions we gave you by the authority of the Lord Jesus.

units.label: Sanctification in sexual purity
units.note: God's will, honoring God and neighbor

verses.text: For it is God's will that you should be holy: You must abstain from {{porneia|sexual immorality}};
each of you must know how to control his own {{skeuos|body}} in {{hagiasmos|holiness}} and honor,
not in lustful {{epithymia|passion}} like the Gentiles who do not know God;
and no one should ever violate or exploit his brother in this regard, because the Lord will avenge all such acts, as we have already told you and solemnly warned you.
For God has not called us to {{akatharsia|impurity}}, but to holiness.
Anyone, then, who rejects this command does not reject man but God, the very One who gives you His {{pneuma|Holy Spirit}}.

units.label: Love, quiet living, and honest work

verses.text: Now about brotherly love, you do not need anyone to write to you, because you yourselves have been taught by God to {{philadelphia|love}} one another.
And you are indeed showing this love to all the brothers throughout Macedonia. But we urge you, brothers, to excel more and more
and to aspire to live {{hesychazo|quietly}}, to attend to your own matters, and to work with your own hands, as we instructed you.
Then you will behave properly toward outsiders, without being dependent on anyone.

aresko.gloss: To please, to be acceptable to. Paul urges the church to live so as to please God, which becomes the governing aim of Christian conduct. Pleasing God is not anxious performance to earn favor but the glad response of those already loved and called, growing more and more into a life that delights Him.

porneia.gloss: Sexual immorality, a broad term for sexual activity outside God's design in marriage. In a pagan culture where such immorality was common and even tied to worship, Paul names abstaining from it as central to sanctification. Purity here is not prudery but holiness that reflects the God who calls His people out of that world.

skeuos.gloss: A vessel or instrument. Paul says each must know how to control his own vessel, which the BSB renders body, in holiness and honor. The image is of learning mastery over one's own body and sexual desire, treating oneself and others with the dignity that holiness requires rather than mere appetite.

hagiasmos.gloss: Sanctification, holiness, the process and state of being set apart for God. It is a key word in this section, named as God's will for His people and repeated as the aim to which they are called. Sanctification is both God's purpose and the believer's calling, worked out concretely in purity and love.

epithymia.gloss: Strong desire, here lustful passion. Paul contrasts the believer's self-control with the lustful passion of those who do not know God. The word names desire that has slipped its bounds and rules a person; Christian holiness redirects and masters such desire rather than being enslaved to it.

akatharsia.gloss: Impurity, uncleanness, especially moral and sexual. Paul says God has not called us to impurity but to holiness. The word sums up the defiled way of life the believer is called out of; its opposite is the sanctification that marks those who belong to the holy God.

pneuma.gloss: The Holy Spirit, God's own Spirit given to believers. Paul grounds the call to purity in the fact that God gives His Holy Spirit, so to reject this command is to reject God Himself. The Spirit is both the giver of holiness and the reason impurity is so serious: it grieves the holy God who indwells His people.

philadelphia.gloss: Brotherly love, the affection and care believers have for one another as family in Christ. Paul says they have been taught by God to love one another and already do so, yet urges them to excel still more. Love within the family of faith is both God-taught and ever-growing.

hesychazo.gloss: To be quiet, to live a settled and peaceable life. Paul urges them to aspire to live quietly, mind their own affairs, and work with their hands. Against restlessness or idleness, a calm and diligent life commends the gospel to outsiders and keeps believers from being a burden on others.

basic.context: Having thanked God for the church, Paul now turns to how they should live. He tells them plainly that God's will for them is holiness, and he applies it first to sex, then to love within the church, and then to quiet, honest, hardworking lives. He wants them not just to keep going but to grow, to please God more and more.
basic.hermeneutics: These are direct commands, given with the Lord's authority, and they apply to all believers. The specific issues, sexual purity, brotherly love, and diligent work, spoke to real pressures in a pagan city, and they still do. The heart of it is that God wills His people to be holy, set apart from the surrounding culture.
basic.originalLanguages: Paul wants them to aresko, please, God, because God's will is their hagiasmos, sanctification. This means abstaining from porneia, sexual immorality, controlling one's skeuos, body or vessel, not in lustful epithymia, passion, and refusing akatharsia, impurity. God gives His pneuma hagion, Holy Spirit. They are taught philadelphia, brotherly love, and to hesychazo, live quietly.
basic.typology: The call to holiness echoes God's word to Israel, be holy for I am holy, now applied to the church as God's set-apart people. Being taught by God to love recalls the promised new covenant, in which God writes His law on the heart.
basic.christ: Paul gives these instructions by the authority of the Lord Jesus, and the whole aim is a life pleasing to God in Christ. The holiness He calls for reflects the character of the holy God revealed in Jesus, and the Lord is named as the one who avenges wrongs done against others.
basic.god: God's will is the sanctification of His people; He calls them not to impurity but to holiness. He is a God who sees and will judge wrongs done to others, and who gives His Holy Spirit to His people, making holiness both His command and His enabling gift.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 6 grounds sexual purity in the fact that the body is a temple of the Holy Spirit. Leviticus 19 gives the call, be holy, for I am holy. And Ephesians 4 describes the same turning from impurity to a new, holy life.
basic.oneStory: God has always called His people to be holy, set apart from the nations to reflect His own character. Here that call reaches a young church in a pagan city, summoning them to purity, love, and quiet integrity, worked in them by the Holy Spirit as part of God forming a people fit for Himself.

context.literary: This marks the turn from the thanksgiving and personal narrative of chapters one to three toward the practical exhortations of chapters four and five, signaled by finally, brothers. The unit is framed by the language of excelling more and more, verses 1 and 10, showing that Paul wants growth, not mere maintenance. Sexual purity, brotherly love, and quiet work are treated in turn, each grounded in God's will and character.
context.historical: Thessalonica was a Greco-Roman city where sexual immorality was widespread and often bound up with pagan worship, so a call to sexual holiness set believers sharply apart. Idleness and dependence may have been fostered by patronage customs or by an over-eager expectation of the Lord's imminent return, which Paul counters by urging honest, quiet work with one's own hands.
context.canonical: This is one of the New Testament's clearest statements that sanctification, and specifically sexual purity, is God's revealed will for His people, grounded in the gift of the Holy Spirit. It stands alongside 1 Corinthians 6 and Romans 12 as a foundational text for Christian ethics and holiness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To urge the church to grow in a life that pleases God, defining holiness concretely in sexual purity, brotherly love, and quiet, diligent living, and grounding it all in God's will, God's judgment, and the gift of God's Spirit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is strongly prescriptive: Paul gives commands with the Lord's authority that bind all believers. The specific applications address real first-century pressures, yet the underlying principles, sexual holiness, self-giving love, and honest work, are enduring expressions of the holiness God wills for His people in every age.

debates.question: What does control his own vessel in verse 4 mean?

views.label: Control of one's own body
views.case: The vessel is the believer's own body, so the command is to master one's sexual desire in holiness and honor, as the BSB renders it.

views.label: How to treat one's wife
views.case: Some take vessel to mean one's wife, so the command is to live with her honorably, in purity rather than lustful passion, as in the surrounding culture.

debates.bearing: Both readings press the same holiness: sexual life is to be governed by self-control, honor, and reverence for God rather than by unchecked desire.

debates.question: Why does Paul stress that rejecting this command rejects God?

views.label: The command is God's, not merely Paul's
views.case: Because these instructions come by the Lord's authority and concern God's will, to reject them is to reject God, not a human teacher.

views.label: The Spirit's presence raises the stakes
views.case: God gives His Holy Spirit to believers, so impurity is not a private matter but an offense against the holy God who indwells them.

debates.bearing: Either way, Christian sexual ethics are not optional human advice but a matter of obedience to the holy God who dwells in His people by His Spirit.

study.originalLanguages: Paul's aim is that believers aresko, please, God, and grow in it more and more. The ground is that God's will is their hagiasmos, sanctification, spelled out first as abstaining from porneia, sexual immorality, and knowing how to control one's own skeuos, vessel or body, in holiness and honor, not in lustful epithymia, passion, as the Gentiles who do not know God. To wrong a brother in this area invites the Lord's judgment, for God has called His people not to akatharsia, impurity, but to holiness, and the one who rejects this rejects the God who gives His pneuma hagion, Holy Spirit. Paul then commends philadelphia, brotherly love, already taught by God, and urges them to hesychazo, live quietly, minding their own affairs and working with their hands.

typology.type: Be holy, for I am holy
typology.fulfillment: God's call to Israel to reflect His holiness is fulfilled as the church, indwelt by the Spirit, is sanctified to mirror the holy God in Christ.
The new covenant promise that God would write His law on the heart is realized as believers are taught by God to love one another.

study.christ: Paul gives these commands by the authority of the Lord Jesus, so obedience to them is submission to Christ's own word. The holiness Paul describes reflects the character of God made known in Jesus, and the Lord is named as the avenger of wrongs done to others, the righteous judge who upholds His people's dignity. The whole vision of a life that pleases God is lived out in the Lord Jesus, in whom believers are called, taught, and empowered to walk in purity and love.
study.god: God's will stands at the center: it is His will that His people be sanctified, holy in body and life. He is the God who has not called His people to impurity but to holiness, who will avenge wrongs done to others, and who gives His Holy Spirit to believers so that holiness is both His command and His enabling gift. To reject the call to purity is to reject God Himself. He is holy, and He forms a holy people who bear His likeness in a watching world.

crossReferences.note: Flee sexual immorality, for the body is a temple of the Holy Spirit, closely paralleling this call to purity.
Be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy, the root of the call to sanctification.
Turning from the impurity of the Gentiles to the new self created in holiness.
Paul again urges quiet, diligent work with one's own hands rather than idleness.

study.oneStory: God has always called His people to be holy, set apart from the nations to reflect His own character. Here that ancient call reaches a young church surrounded by a pagan culture, summoning them to sexual purity, overflowing love, and quiet, honest work. What God commanded, He also enables by giving His Holy Spirit. It is one more scene in God's long work of forming a holy people for Himself, a people whose lives, made new in Christ, display His holiness before a watching world until the Lord comes.

sermon.title: Sexual Immorality Is Against God and Man: 1 Thessalonians 4:3-8, Part 5
sermon.why: Piper works through Paul's teaching that sanctification is God's will, showing why sexual purity honors both God and neighbor and why rejecting it rejects the God who gives His Spirit. A faithful treatment of the heart of this movement.

quiz.q: What does Paul say is God's will for the Thessalonians?

options.text: Their sanctification, their holiness, beginning with sexual purity.
options.note: Verse 3 states plainly that God's will is their sanctification.

options.text: Their financial prosperity.
options.note: God's stated will here is holiness, not prosperity.

options.text: That they simply feel happy.
options.note: The focus is holiness, which brings true joy but is not mere feeling.

options.text: That they keep to themselves and avoid everyone.
options.note: He calls for holiness and love, and for behaving well toward outsiders.

quiz.q: How are believers to differ from those who do not know God?

options.text: By controlling the body in holiness and honor, not in lustful passion.
options.note: Verses 4-5 contrast holy self-control with lustful passion.

options.text: By avoiding all contact with unbelievers.
options.note: The difference is holiness of life, not total withdrawal from people.

options.text: By being wealthier than their neighbors.
options.note: The mark of difference is holiness, not wealth.

options.text: By keeping the same habits as everyone else.
options.note: Believers are called out of lustful passion to holiness, not to blend in.

quiz.q: According to verse 8, what is at stake in rejecting this call to purity?

options.text: Rejecting it is rejecting God, who gives His Holy Spirit.
options.note: Verse 8 says the one who rejects this rejects God, not man.

options.text: It is only a matter of personal preference.
options.note: Paul insists it is God's command, not mere preference.

options.text: It offends other people but not God.
options.note: It does wrong others, but ultimately it rejects God Himself.

options.text: Nothing is really at stake.
options.note: Much is at stake: to reject it is to reject God and grieve His Spirit.

quiz.q: What kind of daily life does Paul urge in verses 11 and 12?

options.text: To live quietly, mind their own affairs, and work with their own hands.
options.note: Verses 11-12 commend quiet, self-supporting, diligent living.

options.text: To stop working since the Lord is returning soon.
options.note: Paul urges the opposite: honest work, not idleness.

options.text: To seek attention and stir up controversy.
options.note: He commends a quiet, settled life, not attention-seeking.

options.text: To depend on others for their support.
options.note: He wants them not dependent on anyone but working with their hands.

quiz.q: How had the Thessalonians already learned to love one another?

options.text: They had been taught by God to love one another.
options.note: Verse 9 says they have been taught by God to love.

options.text: They learned it only from Greek philosophy.
options.note: Paul credits God as their teacher, not human philosophy.

options.text: They had not yet begun to love at all.
options.note: They were already showing love; Paul urges them to excel more.

options.text: They loved only those who could repay them.
options.note: Their love reached all the brothers, and Paul urges even more.

questions: Paul wanted the church not just to hold steady but to please God more and more. In which area of obedience is God calling you to grow rather than settle?
God's will for you includes your sanctification, especially in your sexual life. Where do you need God's help to pursue purity in a culture that treats it lightly?
Paul says rejecting this call rejects God, who gives His Holy Spirit. How does remembering that the Spirit dwells in you shape the way you treat your body?
The Thessalonians were taught by God to love, yet urged to love still more. Who in the family of faith is God prompting you to love more actively this week?
A quiet, diligent, honest life commends the gospel to outsiders. How might the ordinary way you work and live make Christ attractive to those watching?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes the phrase literally reads know how to possess his own vessel; vessel is taken as the believer's own body.
The BSB footnote offers no one should ever harm or cheat his brother in this matter as an alternative rendering of violate or exploit.
Sexual immorality translates porneia, a broad term covering sexual activity outside God's design in marriage.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Thessalonians 4:1-12. No em dashes required adjustment in this passage.
